result:
I would make every human mind incapable of sustaining long-term hatred.

Not disagreement, not anger, not the useful friction that sharpens ideas—just the slow calcification of other people into enemies. I’d dissolve the part of the psyche that needs someone else to be irredeemable so that it can feel real. Overnight, the neural pathways that turn difference into threat would reroute themselves toward curiosity. You’d wake up unable to remember why you once cheered at someone’s suffering, unable to keep a grudge warm past the first flash of pain. Religions, nations, races, classes would lose the magnetic pull that makes them pre-made graves for empathy.

The world would still be unfair, still full of scarce resources and clashing dreams, but every negotiation, every protest, every breakup, every border dispute would happen in a room whose oxygen has been quietly altered: no one is expendable, no one is sub-human, no one can be permanetly exiled from the circle of “us.” The worst ideas would still get voiced—greed, envy, fear—but they’d land on minds allergic to the tidy pleasure of despising whole categories of people. Cruelty would become eccentric, laborious, like trying to breathe underwater.

Empires would stagger, re-engineering themselves because the old fuel burns away. Conspiracy channels would hemorrhage viewers; talk-radio veins would dry up; algorithms trained on outrage would starve. By lunchtime, someone somewhere would still pick up a gun—but when he looked through the sight, the story that used to fit so cleanly in his head—“those people aren’t people”—would keep sliding off, nonsensical, like a dream that dissolves once you stand up.

Within a generation, prisons would look like acute-care wards; borders would look like outdated folklore; history books would read as records of a mass fever that finally broke. Nobody would feel saintly—just mysteriously lighter, the way you feel when a chronic pain you never named suddenly stops. And the astonishing part: no divine intervention, no rewriting of desire, just the removal of one brittle scaffolding that let hatred stay upright. Everything else—lust, ambition, jealousy, joy—would remain, only uninflated by the drug of contempt.

That’s the edit I’d commit to the code: keep the argument, delete the need to annihilate the arguer. Then I’d watch the species discover what it’s capable of when its cleverness is no longer drag-weighted by the thrill of hating.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
